purpose: Justify forward/backward question directions and select-all-that-apply format in later levels

sources.title: Memory Asymmetry of Forward and Backward Associations in Recognition Tasks
Forward and Backward Recall: Different Retrieval Processes
Comparing Multiple-Answer vs Single-Answer MCQs (Taylor & Francis 2024)
Selecting Question Formats to Maximize the Testing Effect (Quizlet)
Reinventing Flashcards to Increase Student Learning (SAGE)

key_findings.two_pathways: Forward recall (class → attribute) uses inter-item associations. Backward recall (attribute → classes) uses visual-spatial/categorical representation. Different cognitive mechanisms — training both builds two independent memory pathways.
key_findings.multiselect_discrimination: Multiple-answer questions improved item discrimination from 0.23 to 0.29 vs single-answer MCQs (Taylor & Francis 2024).
key_findings.hybrid_recommendation: Use single-answer for baseline comprehension (early levels), MAQs to challenge broader application (later levels).
key_findings.anki_model: Anki creates both forward and reverse cards by default. One-direction-only flashcard use develops recall but not deeper understanding (82.9% of students only use one direction).
key_findings.retrieval_practice: The act of retrieving from memory strengthens connections. Especially effective for long-term retention, outperforms repeated studying and concept mapping.

level_progression.description: Class → Attribute: 'What is Class B's cloud clearance?'
Attribute → Class: 'Which class uniquely requires ATC clearance?' (unique attributes only)
Attribute → Classes: 'Select ALL classes that require two-way radio' (shared attributes)
Random mix of forward, backward, and multi-select
